title: Programmed Links
text: Programmed Links If you want to define links with multiple fields or even multiple occurrences, you must use a programmed link. This is because it is not possible to select more than one field in Uniface for copying to the Microsoft Windows clipboard. The exact syntax for defining a programmed link is different for every client application. Some applications use a formula in a field or a cell, others use macros. An example of a programmed link in a spreadsheet program: {=IDE|FORM!"FIELD1,FIELD2.ENTITY[5:10]"} Copy The same example in a word processing application could be: {DDEAUTO IDE FORM FIELD1,FIELD2.ENTITY[5:10]/*MERGEFORMAT} Copy All DDE applications have the service, topic, and item names requirement in common. For this reason, you should refer to your application documentation for information on how to establish a programmed link. Related Links DDE Links Applications that communiicate via DDE do so using links that can be defined by copying and pasting them to the destination application or programming them using the DDE syntax rules. DDE Syntax Rules Uniface as a DDE Server When Uniface acts as a DDE server, the DDE link must be defined in the client application. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
